story:
Leighton, a week past signing his divorce papers and fighting the pull of a drink he hadn’t taken yet, watched a red Audi S5 take a curve too fast, spin out, and flip into a ditch. He braked his gray Subaru, jumped out, and ran toward the wreck. Gasoline hung in the dusk air. Peering in, he saw only one person inside—a driver, no passengers. That felt like luck, as far as luck went.

“Hello there? You awake?” he called. Maybe a mumble, maybe not. He circled to the driver’s side, saw temporary tags on a brand-new car already ruined, and tried the door. An oak had the driver’s side pinned, the car tilted just enough to make crossing over to the passenger side possible. Something flashed near the rear of the car—maybe a spark, maybe his fear—but he took it as a warning. He hurried around.

He yanked the passenger handle. Locked. He hammered the window with his palms. Inside, the driver’s hair was short and blonde, head lolling. In the fading light, he couldn’t get a clear look at her face. He pictured some kid with too much car and not enough practice. “Miss! Wake up! You have to unlock the door!” No response. No blood that he could see, but she might be out cold. He banged harder, then stopped and shouted. Movement, slow as syrup.

“That’s it… keep going… you can do it,” he coaxed. She crawled toward the passenger side like someone moving through a bad dream. He thought, unwelcome and familiar, of how his ex-wife had put off everything—bills, licenses, groceries—except the divorce. She’d sprinted toward that. He shoved the memory away. Focus. Another flicker at the rear. His heart pounded. He slammed the glass in time with it.

She finally reached across. “No! Flip the unlock switch. Unlock the door!” he shouted. Fingers fumbling. A blessed clunk. Leighton tore the door open, grabbed her, and dragged her out, hauling her ten feet from the wreck. He closed his eyes in relief, then looked down into her face.

“Hello, Leighton,” she breathed.

Afterward, he got his ex-wife an ambulance. She healed fast—broken clavicle, concussion—and the wrecked Audi was replaced by another, folded into the costs he was already covering after the split. Last he heard, she’d had a child with another man and didn’t see the point of marriage anymore. He wanted to hate her. He told himself not to. Other people had it worse. He’d been telling himself that a lot.

That night, he cracked the Maker’s Mark he’d bought and hadn’t touched. He took a long drink, not triumph, not celebration—just a dull warmth filling a space that rescue and relief hadn’t. He reminded himself he was lucky. He was alive. He had done the right thing when it counted. Somewhere, people were bearing heavier loads than his. He held on to that thought, even as the burn slid down and settled in.
